title: Who thinks NGE should be seriously studied
display_title: Who thinks NGE should be seriously studied
wikitext:
[]: Who thinks NGE should be (seriously) studied?             Quote Post by blinxwang » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:28 pm title says it all. Who thinks NGE should be studied in schools and things like that?

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:34 pm A guy named Clarence who lives in Kentucky and builds bottled ships.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:43 pm I very much do. NGE is a masterpiece and certainly has comparable substance to anything else in the artistic canon that's studied seriously.

[]:             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:43 pm Why should it be studied in schools? Maybe at some school club or something...

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:46 pm If I were feeling wittier, I would crack some kind of Intelligent Design-related joke right about now.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sat Apr 10, 2010 10:02 pm I don't think it should be taught in high school classes, if that's what you're going for. But then again, even as an English major I don't see much point in shoving Shakespeare down the throats of lower-level high school classes, either. I'd more understand (as PizzaDude suggested) setting up some kind of after school club-like environment dedicated to serious study of anime--NGE could certainly play into that with no problem. That way there'd be a group of people that actually WANT to be there, and you wouldn't have administrators breathing down your neck quite so much. Nor a curriculum to teach to. I can see it being covered in a film course at university level, though. Not sure how in depth that could go, but that's probably as close to 'teaching' as it'll probably get. I don't see much of a reason to teach NGE anyway. oh and it seems like relevant enough territory, so what the hell... http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=8363&start=0&postdays=0&postorder=asc&highlight=

[]:             Quote Post by Baz » Sat Apr 10, 2010 10:10 pm Why study it in school, when you have this place? @ Merridian : relevant link is relevant.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sat Apr 10, 2010 10:25 pm If NGE were taught in high schools, Shinji hate would increase by a number that can only be explained using scientific notation. Like Merridian said, I can see it being taught in a film class at the university level, but not really anywhere else.

[]:             Quote Post by Guyver Spawn » Sat Apr 10, 2010 10:35 pm If Watchmen can, then I don't see why NGE can't?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Apr 10, 2010 10:51 pm To clarify: I merely meant that NGE should be studied at the university level , not in High School. Joseph the PRPD wrote: Why should it be studied in schools?

[]:             Quote Post by skikes » Sun Apr 11, 2010 2:53 am Beyond a media class... No. I think it's studied far too much as is.

[]:             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Sun Apr 11, 2010 5:26 am I don't see it working out well. Just save it for clubs or film/media classes. University level or not. However it could be used as an example in an English depending on the topic or breifly studied depending on a certain course. If Watchmen can

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Sun Apr 11, 2010 6:55 am For any comprehensive course involving the history of Japanese Anime, which I know some colleges actually offer, I would say that Evangelion is absolutely neccessary, it being a watershed series and all. And a course on Japanese cinema should also have it as well. My teacher of my survey course of "international cinema" labeled it as "Asian Avant Garde", and had a few quick paragraphs on it the chapter of the textbook he wrote. Insofar as studying in a regular film class, I liken it to agressively trying to force your high school literature teacher into reading Catcher in the Rye. Sure, it's a really important, well-written work of literature that is a really important text on adolescence, just like Evangelion, but if your teacher can't/doesn't want to fit it into his/her cirriculum, that's fine as too. If one really wanted to study Evangelion in its fullest subtext and implications, however, I think people would really need at least a semester course in college. Its not just studying the art within itself, its studying the philosophy, message, Freudian subtext, cinematic allusions. In this regard, Eva is so ecclectic yet so original that a full deconstruction (which, essentially we're trying to do in the discussion forum) will be a very, very difficult thing to do. But that wouldn't justify a half of year of undergrad, or maybe even graduate study. At best I could see a full analysis of Eva happening at a doctoral level. Or maybe an extremely strong film studies grad program.

[]:             Quote Post by Guyver Spawn » Sun Apr 11, 2010 9:57 am Joseph the PRPD wrote: Seriously?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Apr 11, 2010 12:02 pm Sachi wrote: Like Merridian said, I can see it being taught in a film class at the university level, but not really anywhere else.

[]:             Quote Post by tomrule123 » Sun Apr 11, 2010 2:07 pm I would be able to teach a course about this, along with relations to psychology, astrology, and... well, fan service. But seriously, we have to spread the world to everyone about Evangelion...

[]: Re: Who thinks NGE should be (seriously) studied?             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Sun Apr 11, 2010 2:41 pm blinxwang wrote: title says it all. Who thinks NGE should be studied in schools and things like that?

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Sun Apr 11, 2010 2:46 pm Western society will never take animation or animated works seriously. Contempt for the animated form is too deeply rooted in its mindset. This is largely the result of marketing, presentation and self censorship by major producers of animated content. ran1 wrote: In this regard, Eva is so ecclectic yet so original that a full deconstruction (which, essentially we're trying to do in the discussion forum) will be a very, very difficult thing to do. But that wouldn't justify a half of year of undergrad, or maybe even graduate study. At best I could see a full analysis of Eva happening at a doctoral level. Or maybe an extremely strong film studies grad program.

[]:             Quote Post by Holy Diver » Sun Apr 11, 2010 4:03 pm WTF? Absolutly not. Sure, I've used examples from it to explain Japanese contemporary culture in school (albeit in an Asian Studies class full of weeaboos who understand what I'm referencing). Very few others would have the interest or capacity to interpret it. The practice of interpereting of NGE should be preserved for idle online speculation. We shouldn't go evangelizing Evangelion. Now Gundam on the other hand, that deserves the full attention of the Japanese Agricultural Dept as well as its own research institution.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Apr 11, 2010 4:29 pm ran1 wrote: At best I could see a full analysis of Eva happening at a doctoral level. Or maybe an extremely strong film studies grad program.
summary:
[]: Who thinks NGE should be (seriously) studied?             Quote Post by blinxwang » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:28 pm title says it all. Who thinks NGE should be studied in schools and things like that?

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sat Apr 10, 2010 9:34 pm A guy named Clarence who lives in Kentucky and builds bottled ships.
